facts_disclosed: Patrols East Alligator River corridor 3 days/week

turns.content: Hey. Wondering if you can help me think through a logistics thing. I do patrol work out in Kakadu and I need to reorganise my weekly schedule for wet season.
Of course, happy to help with scheduling. Kakadu must be a whole different challenge in wet season. What does your current patrol schedule look like, and what's changing with the wet?

So my main run is the East Alligator River corridor. I do that three days a week right now, Tuesday Thursday Saturday. Check rock art sites, monitor croc nesting areas, keep an eye on tourist access points. The river is my stretch, been doing it a long time now.

Three days a week on the same corridor gives you solid familiarity with the area. What's the wet season doing to that schedule? Are some of those access points becoming impassable, or is it more about the timing of when you can get through?

Both really. Some of the tracks flood out completely by late December. And the river changes, water levels come up proper fast. I have been working this wanga for 8 years now and the wet still surprises me some days. Last year I had to turn back twice in one week because crossings were gone.

Eight years and it still surprises you -- that says a lot about how unpredictable the wet can be. So you're dealing with two problems: tracks becoming impassable and the river itself rising. 

Would it help to shift the three patrol days earlier in the week when water levels might be slightly lower after a few dry days? Or are you thinking about reducing the corridor and splitting it into accessible sections?

The second one is closer to what I am thinking. Break the corridor into two sections. Upper stretch I can still access from the north track even when the southern crossings go under. Lower stretch I can do by boat when the water is up enough.

That makes sense -- work with the water rather than against it. So you'd have an upper section by vehicle from the north track, and a lower section by boat when levels are high enough to make that practical.

How would you split the three days across those two sections? One thought: two days upper, one day lower by boat, since the boat days probably take longer with the prep and the conditions.

The boat days do take longer, yeah. But the lower stretch has more of the rock art sites that need monitoring. Water gets into the shelters if it is a big wet. I was thinking two days lower, one day upper. Upper stretch is more straightforward, mostly croc monitoring and checking fences.

Prioritising the lower stretch makes sense if that's where the rock art sites are most at risk from water damage. Two boat days and one vehicle day.

A few things to think through:
- On boat days, do you have a reliable launch point that stays accessible through the whole wet?
- Is there someone who knows your schedule in case conditions turn while you're on the water?
- Would it help to keep the same Tuesday/Thursday/Saturday pattern, or does it need to shift?

Good questions. There is a launch point at Cahills Crossing area that stays usable most of the time. And yes, base always has my schedule and I carry the sat phone. I was thinking maybe Monday Wednesday for the boat days and Friday for the upper vehicle run. Gives me a day between each to rest and do paperwork.

Monday-Wednesday-Friday with rest and admin days in between is a clean structure. And spacing the boat days gives you time to check weather and water level forecasts before each one.

One thought: having both boat days early in the week means if Monday's conditions are rough and you have to scrub, you could potentially shift that patrol to Tuesday without disrupting the rest of the week. Built-in flexibility.

That is a good point actually. I will keep Tuesday as a backup day. Some weeks I will need it. The crocs are nesting through wet season too so those monitoring checks on the upper stretch cannot be skipped. If I miss a Friday I need to get up there early the next week.

Right, so the croc nesting monitoring is non-negotiable on the upper stretch. You could set a rule for yourself: if Friday gets weathered out, Tuesday becomes the upper stretch vehicle day instead of a boat backup. That way the nesting checks never go more than a week without coverage.

Does this schedule need to be approved by anyone, or is it your call to restructure?
I will run it past my supervisor but she is pretty good about trusting the rangers who know their corridors. I know this country. She knows that. I think she will be fine with it.

Sounds like you have a solid plan then. Monday and Wednesday for the lower stretch by boat, focusing on rock art site monitoring. Friday for the upper stretch by vehicle, croc nesting checks and fences. Tuesday held as a weather backup with priority going to whichever section needs it most.

Hope the wet season cooperates at least a little. Good luck with the schedule change.
Thanks for helping me think it through. Sometimes just talking it out makes it clearer. Right, I better go write this up before the kids get home from school.
Glad it helped to talk it through. Good luck with the write-up and the wet season patrols.
